abstract: Supervised machine learning traditionally involves classifying novel data points based on a foundation of pre-labeled training examples. In this work, we demonstrate that the support vector machine—a powerful and widely used binary classifier—can be implemented on a quantum architecture with a complexity that scales logarithmically with both the vector dimensionality and the size of the training set. This represents an exponential speedup over classical sampling algorithms that require polynomial time. The cornerstone of this quantum "big data" approach is a novel nonsparse matrix exponentiation technique, which enables the efficient inversion of the training data’s inner-product (kernel) matrix.
